1: A pattern: “Raise up a lament for the princes of Israel.” Ezekiel the prophet is commanded to lament over Israel’s failed kings. Christ himself laments over Jerusalem and its leadership: “O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those sent to her! How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood, and you were not willing” (Matthew 23:37). The prophetic lament over Israel’s failed shepherds (Ezekiel 19, 34) reaches its fullest expression in Christ’s tears over the city (Luke 19:41).
2: A type: The lioness who bears royal cubs “among lions” — Judah as a lioness — points forward to the Davidic promise that culminates in Christ. Genesis 49:9-10 (“Judah is a lion’s cub... the scepter shall not depart from Judah”) is fulfilled in “the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David” who has conquered (Revelation 5:5). Where the lioness’s cubs are taken captive and the royal line apparently ends, Christ is the cub who cannot be captured and whose kingdom cannot end (Luke 1:33).
3: A structural note: The first cub “learned to catch prey and devoured men” — the predatory kingship that characterizes Israel’s failed rulers. Christ comes as the anti-predatory king: “the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many” (Mark 10:45). The lions who devour men are replaced by the Shepherd-King who lays down his life for the flock.
4: A pattern: “He was caught in their pit; they brought him in chains to the land of Egypt.” The captured and chained king is the type of Israel’s humiliated rulers. Christ is the king who surrendered himself to captors: “do you think I cannot call on my Father, and he will at once put at my disposal more than twelve legions of angels?” (Matthew 26:53). He was bound and taken away not by enemies’ power but by his own voluntary submission — the captured king who could not be held (Acts 2:24: “God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it”).
5: A structural note: “When she saw that her hope was gone and her waiting had come to nothing, she took another of her cubs.” The disappointed hope and renewed attempt is the pattern of Israel’s persistent seeking of the messianic king through the Davidic line — each king disappointing, each captivity crushing the hope, yet the hope renewed. The final renewal of this hope comes not through another failed cub but through the resurrection: “I had hoped that he was the one to redeem Israel” (Luke 24:21) is transformed into “the Lord has risen indeed” (Luke 24:34).
6: A structural note: The second cub also “learned to catch prey and devoured men” — the pattern of predatory kingship repeating across generations. The repeated failure of Israel’s kings creates the vacuum that Christ fills: where every human king devoured, the king who gave himself to be devoured for others establishes a radically different kingdom. “I am the good shepherd; the good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep” (John 10:11).
7: A structural note: “He ravaged their strongholds and laid waste their cities.” The destructive king who wastes the cities is the anti-type of the king who builds the city: “I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it” (Matthew 16:18). Christ is the builder-king whose construction is permanent, contrasted with every Israelite king who proved a destroyer.
8: A structural note: The nations gathering against the lion-king, catching him in their pit — the international opposition that ends Israel’s monarchy. Psalm 2 describes the same international gathering: “the kings of the earth set themselves, and the rulers take counsel together, against the LORD and against his Anointed.” Christ’s trial before Pilate (a Roman ruler) and the Sanhedrin (Israel’s leaders) is the ultimate gathering of nations against the LORD’s Anointed — fulfilling what Ps 2 predicts and what Ezekiel’s lament mourns.
9: A type: “So that his voice would no longer be heard on the mountains of Israel.” The silencing of the lion-king’s voice is the lament’s epitaph. Christ’s voice, silenced at the cross, was heard again from the empty tomb — and will be heard one final time when “the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God” (1 Thessalonians 4:16). The silenced voice of the Davidic king is not permanently stilled but raised in resurrection proclamation.
10: A type: “Your mother was like a vine transplanted beside the waters.” Israel as a fruitful vine points forward to Christ’s own vine-identity: “I am the true vine” (John 15:1). The difference is decisive: Ezekiel’s vine is the nation Israel, which fails; Christ is the “true” (alēthinos) vine — the one in whom all the vine-imagery of the OT is finally and genuinely embodied. The false or failed vines of Ezekiel’s laments are the negative context that makes Christ’s self-identification as the True Vine intelligible.
11: A type: “She had strong branches that served as rulers’ scepters.” The scepter-branch is the Davidic royal staff (Genesis 49:10: “the scepter shall not depart from Judah”). Christ is the Branch (tsemach, Zechariah 6:12) and the scepter-rod of Psalms 2:9 with which he rules the nations. The strong scepter-branches of the vine represent the Davidic dynasty at its peak; their destruction in the lament creates the expectation for the one indestructible Branch whose rule is eternal.
12: A type: “The east wind dried up her fruit; her strong branches broke and withered; fire consumed them.” The east-wind destruction of the vine anticipates the judgment that reaches its climax in the crucifixion: Christ the True Vine is stripped bare, the branches torn off, the vine seemingly destroyed. But “unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit” (John 12:24) — the death of the vine in Ezekiel 19 is the type of the death that produces the harvest of Ezekiel 15 of John.
13: A type: “Now she is planted in the wilderness — in a dry and thirsty land.” The vine exiled to the waterless desert is the condition Christ entered in the incarnation: “the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head” (Matthew 8:20). The wilderness planting of the vine reverses only when the river of life flows from the new temple (Ezekiel 47:1-12) — which Christ is: the one from whom living water flows (John 7:38-39). The desert exile is temporary; the replanting beside the river of life is permanent.
14: A type: “Fire has spread from one of her own branches and devoured her fruit — she has no strong branch left, no scepter to rule with. This is a lament, and it shall be used as a lament.” The lament’s climax — no scepter remains — is the OT’s honest acknowledgment that the Davidic dynasty’s human form has ended. But the lament itself encodes the hope: a lament that prescribes its own future liturgical use anticipates an audience that will sing it with eschatological awareness. Christ turns the lament into a song of triumph: the Lion of Judah holds the scepter that no human king could keep (Revelation 5:5).
